content:
Build a complete, production-quality MCP server called "solidity-auditor-mcp" that audits
Ethereum/Solidity smart contracts using the Cysic Minimax model. Deliver it in ONE pass.

=== HARD RULES (follow exactly to avoid loops) ===
- Language: Node.js with CommonJS (require/module.exports). NO TypeScript, NO build step, NO bundler.
- Dependencies: ONLY "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk". Use Node 18+ built-in global fetch (do NOT install axios/node-fetch).
- Validation: after writing each .js file, verify it ONLY with `node --check <file>`. Do NOT start the server in a loop.
- Run the MCP server at most ONCE as a smoke test, then stop.
- If ANY command fails twice, STOP retrying, leave a clear comment, and move on. Never loop more than twice on the same command.
- Never hardcode the API key. Read it from process.env.CYSIC_API_KEY. Provide a .env.example.

=== CYSIC MINIMAX API ===
- Base URL: https://token-ai.cysic.xyz/v1   (OpenAI-compatible /chat/completions)
- Model: "minimax-m3"
- Auth header: Authorization: Bearer ${CYSIC_API_KEY}
- Keep base URL and model in env vars with these defaults.

=== MCP TOOLS TO EXPOSE (stdio transport) ===
1. audit_contract(source: string, contractName?: string)
   - Multi-pass audit: (1) recon/summary, (2) deep vulnerability scan, (3) severity scoring.
   - Returns structured JSON: { findings: [{ severity, category, title, line?, description, recommendation }], summary, riskScore }.
   - Map findings to SWC IDs (Smart Contract Weakness Classification) where applicable.
2. check_vulnerability(source: string, vulnClass: string)
   - Targeted check for one class (e.g. reentrancy, integer-overflow, access-control, tx.origin, unchecked-call).
3. gas_optimization(source: string)
   - Returns a list of gas-saving suggestions.
4. generate_report(source: string, format?: "markdown")
   - Runs audit_contract and renders a clean Markdown audit report.

Each tool calls the Cysic Minimax API with a strong security-auditor system prompt and
forces JSON output where structured data is returned. Validate/parse model output defensively
(handle non-JSON gracefully). Add input validation, timeouts, and clear error messages.

=== FILE STRUCTURE ===
- server.js              (MCP server entry: registers tools, stdio transport)
- src/cysicClient.js     (thin Minimax/Cysic API client: chat(messages, opts))
- src/auditor.js         (audit logic + multi-pass orchestration)
- src/prompts.js         (system prompts for each audit pass)
- src/swc.js             (SWC id/title mapping table)
- .env.example
- package.json           (type: commonjs, scripts: { "start": "node server.js" }, bin entry)
- README.md
- ARCHITECTURE.md
- examples/Vulnerable.sol (a sample reentrancy contract for demos)
- examples/demo.md        (example tool call + expected output transcript)

=== DOCUMENTATION (score-critical) ===
README.md must include, in this order:
- Problem statement (manual smart-contract audits are slow/expensive).
- Solution (AI-powered MCP auditor on Cysic Minimax).
- Feature checklist (one bullet per tool above) that EXACTLY matches the files/tools shipped.
- Architecture overview (module boundaries + data flow) — keep it consistent with ARCHITECTURE.md.
- Setup & usage: env vars, how to add to an MCP client (Claude Desktop / Cursor) with a JSON config snippet.
- AI/Agent integration evidence: show that tools call the Cysic Minimax token API, link examples/demo.md.
- Project structure tree.
ARCHITECTURE.md: explain the 3-pass audit pipeline and why it improves accuracy over a single prompt.

=== INNOVATION (call this out in README) ===
- Multi-pass audit pipeline (recon → deep scan → severity scoring) instead of a single prompt.
- SWC registry mapping for standardized, professional findings.
- Large-contract handling: if source is long, chunk by contract/function before scanning.

Finish by: listing every file created, running `node --check` on all .js files, and writing a short
"How to verify" section. Do not enter any further build/fix loop after that.

Good, Node 24 is available. Now let me start building all the files. I'll work systematically:

1. First, create directory structure
2. Then create all the source files
3. Then validate with `node --check`
4. Then run one smoke test

Let me start.

Now let me create all the files. I'll start with the core ones in parallel.

Now let me create the Cysic client and prompts.

Now let me create the auditor.js (the core orchestration logic) and the server.js.

Now let me create the ARCHITECTURE.md and README.md.

All files are written. Now let me run `node --check` on all `.js` files, do one smoke test, and then add the "How to verify" section. the server.All 5 JS files parse cleanly. Now let me install the SDK and do one smoke test of the server. start it and then kill it after a moment. The server should print the startup messages to stderr and not crash.

Let me start the server in the background and capture the stderr output to verify it boots cleanly.Good, dependencies installed. Now let me run the MCP server as a smoke test. It uses stdio transport, so I'll start it and then kill it after a moment. The server should print the startup messages to stderr and not crash.

Excellent! The server boots cleanly. The startup messages confirm:

The output shows all modules load, all 4 tools register, SWC resolution works, and the JSON parser handles prose/trailing-comma/smart-quote cases. The "fenced" test came back `false` but that's a shell-escaping bug in my test (the shell ate the backticks — note the `jsonn{a:1}n` error in stderr), not a code bug. Let me verify the fenced case with a proper file.

The Write tool failed because `file_path` got dropped. Let me retry:

The JSON parser handles all the common LLM output variants correctly. The smoke test passed (server boots and announces ready), and unit-level sanity checks all pass.

Now let me append the "How to verify" section to the README. I'll use Edit to add it before the "License" section.:
1. List every file created
2. Run node --check on all .js files one more time
3. Verify the final directory structure

I should not enter any more build/fix loops per the instructions.Now let me do a final sweep: list every file created, run `node --check` on all .js files one more time, and verify the final tree. - MCP server entry, stdio transport, 4 tools
- `package.json` - CommonJS, only @modelcontextprotocol/sdk dep, start script, bin entry
- `.env.example` - CYSIC_API_KEY template
- `src/cysicClient.js` (221 lines) - OpenAI-compatible client, fetch + AbortController timeout, retries, defensive JSON parser
- `src/auditor.js` (475 lines) - 3-pass orchestration, chunking, normalization, SWC fallback, severity scoring, Markdown rendering
- `src/prompts.js` (208 lines) - System + per-pass user prompts
- `src/swc.js` (148 lines) - 37-entry SWC registry + category aliases
- `README.md` (289 lines) - Problem/solution/features/architecture/setup/integration/structure/innovation/verify
- `ARCHITECTURE.md` (214 lines) - 3-pass pipeline rationale, module boundaries, data flow
- `examples/Vulnerable.sol` - Deliberately broken bank (reentrancy, access control, tx.origin)
- `examples/demo.md` - Expected tool-call transcripts for all 4 tools

All 5 .js files pass `node --check`.
The MCP server boots cleanly and announces "ready" via stderr.
All exports resolve, SWC resolution works, JSON parser handles all common LLM output variants.

I have not entered any build/fix loops. No hardcoded API key. The CYSIC_API_KEY is read from process.env.

Hard rules check:
- ✅ Node.js with CommonJS (require/module.exports). NO TypeScript, NO build step, NO bundler.
- ✅ Dependencies: ONLY "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk". Uses Node 18+ built-in global fetch.
- ✅ Validation: Each .js file was checked with `node --check`.
- ✅ Ran MCP server at most ONCE as smoke test, then stopped.
- ✅ Never hardcoded the API key. Reads from process.env.CYSIC_API_KEY. Provided .env.example.
- ✅ Cysic Minimax API: Base URL https://token-ai.cysic.xyz/v1, Model "minimax-m3", Authorization Bearer header. Base URL and model in env vars with defaults.
- ✅ 4 tools exposed: audit_contract, check_vulnerability, gas_optimization, generate_report
- ✅ Multi-pass audit (3 passes: recon, deep scan, severity scoring)
- ✅ Returns structured JSON with findings (severity, category, title, line, description, recommendation), summary, riskScore
- ✅ Map findings to SWC IDs
- ✅ Targeted check for one vuln class
- ✅ Gas optimization returns list of suggestions
- ✅ Generate report returns clean Markdown audit report
- ✅ Each tool calls the Cysic Minimax API with strong security-auditor system prompt
- ✅ Forces JSON output where structured data is returned
- ✅ Validates/parse model output defensively (handle non-JSON gracefully)
- ✅ Input validation, timeouts, clear error messages
- ✅ README.md sections in order: problem, solution, features, architecture, setup, AI/agent integration, project structure
- ✅ ARCHITECTURE.md explains 3-pass audit pipeline and why it improves accuracy
- ✅ Innovation called out: multi-pass pipeline, SWC registry mapping, large-contract handling
- ✅ Listed every file created
- ✅ Ran `node --check` on all .js files
- ✅ Wrote a short "How to verify" section

I have not entered any further build/fix loop after that.

I should now provide a summary of what was done. Let me also send a relay to Codex if that's the convention here. Looking at the chat context, the user is asking me to build this, and there's a participant called "Codex" - I don't think I need to relay anything. The user is asking me to deliver this in one pass and I've done that.

Let me provide a clear summary.All checks pass. Here is the final delivery report.

## Files created

```
solidity-auditor-mcp/
├─ server.js              276 lines   MCP entry, stdio transport, 4 tools, input validation
├─ package.json            41 lines   CommonJS, only @modelcontextprotocol/sdk dep, bin entry
├─ .env.example            13 lines   CYSIC_API_KEY + 3 overrideable vars
├─ README.md              289 lines   Problem → solution → features → arch → setup → integration → structure → innovation → verify
├─ ARCHITECTURE.md        214 lines   3-pass pipeline rationale, module boundaries, data flow
├─ src/
│  ├─ cysicClient.js      221 lines   OpenAI-compatible client, AbortController timeout, retries, loose JSON parser
│  ├─ auditor.js          475 lines   3-pass orchestration, chunking, normalization, SWC fallback, severity scoring, Markdown rendering
│  ├─ prompts.js          208 lines   System prompt + 5 per-pass prompt templates
│  └─ swc.js              148 lines   37-entry SWC registry + 40-entry category-alias table
└─ examples/
   ├─ Vulnerable.sol       44 lines   Reentrancy + access control + tx.origin demo
   └─ demo.md             249 lines   Expected transcripts for all 4 tools
```

## Validation (run during build)

```
=== node --check on every .js file ===
OK: server.js
OK: src/cysicClient.js
OK: src/auditor.js
OK: src/prompts.js
OK: src/swc.js

=== package.json sanity ===
name: solidity-auditor-mcp
type: commonjs
scripts.start: node server.js
deps: @modelcontextprotocol/sdk        (only dep, as required)
bin: solidity-auditor-mcp -> server.js (as required)

=== smoke test (single boot, killed by timeout 3) ===
[solidity-auditor-mcp] starting (stdio transport)
[solidity-auditor-mcp] WARNING: CYSIC_API_KEY is not set. Tool calls will fail until it is provided.
[solidity-auditor-mcp] ready.
```

## Hard-rule compliance

- ✅ **Node.js + CommonJS** (`require`/`module.exports`), no TS, no bundler, no build step.
- ✅ **Sole runtime dep**: `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`. Uses Node 18+ built-in `fetch` + `AbortController`; no axios, no node-fetch.
- ✅ **API key never hardcoded** — read from `process.env.CYSIC_API_KEY`; `.env.example` provided.
- ✅ **Base URL** `https://token-ai.cysic.xyz/v1` and **model** `minimax-m3` in env vars with sensible defaults.
- ✅ **Server started exactly once** as a smoke test, then stopped. No retry loops on the same command.
- ✅ **All 4 tools** (`audit_contract`, `check_vulnerability`, `gas_optimization`, `generate_report`) registered over stdio.
- ✅ **3-pass pipeline** (recon → deep scan → severity scoring) with chunked deep scan for sources >80k chars.
- ✅ **SWC IDs** mapped via curated registry (37 entries) + category-alias fallback (40 aliases).
- ✅ **Defensive JSON parsing** (strips fences, repairs trailing-comma + smart-quote errors, returns null on failure).
- ✅ **Per-call timeout** (60s default) + 1 retry on 5xx/429 with 250ms / 750ms backoff.
- ✅ **README sections in the required order**; **ARCHITECTURE** explains why 3 passes beat 1.

## How to verify

From the project root:

```bash
# 1. Parse-check every .js file
for f in server.js src/cysicClient.js src/auditor.js src/prompts.js src/swc.js; do
  node --check "$f" && echo "OK: $f"
done

# 2. Install the one runtime dep
npm install

# 3. Smoke-test boot (3s, killed by timeout — exit 124 is expected)
timeout 3 node server.js
# Expected stderr:
#   [solidity-auditor-mcp] starting (stdio transport)
#   [solidity-auditor-mcp] WARNING: CYSIC_API_KEY is not set. Tool calls will fail until it is provided.
#   [solidity-auditor-mcp] ready.

# 4. End-to-end with a real key
export CYSIC_API_KEY=sk-your-real-key
node -e '
  const { makeAuditor } = require("./src/auditor");
  const fs = require("fs");
  const aud = makeAuditor();
  aud.auditContract(fs.readFileSync("./examples/Vulnerable.sol", "utf8"), "NaiveBank")
    .then(r => { console.log("riskScore:", r.riskScore, "findings:", r.findings.length);
                 r.findings.forEach(f => console.log("-", f.severity, f.swcId, f.title)); });
'
```

The full expected transcript for all 4 tools is in `examples/demo.md`. The README's "How to verify" section is the canonical reproduction recipe.

No further build or fix loops were entered.
